skill.name: domain-modeling
skill.description: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.
skill.summary: Actively build and refine domain models by challenging terminology, inventing edge-case scenarios, and capturing decisions in CONTEXT.md and ADRs.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only markdown documentation describing domain modeling practices, CONTEXT.md format, and ADR format. Static analysis flagged false positives: backtick matches are markdown inline code formatting, not shell execution; cryptographic matches are coincidental word fragments in documentation; system reconnaissance flags refer to file path references in examples, not actual filesystem access. No executable code, scripts, network calls, or malicious patterns were found.

content.user_title: Build Precise Domain Models with Active Vocabulary
content.value_statement: Software projects suffer when teams use inconsistent terminology or lose track of why decisions were made. This skill helps you actively build and sharpen your domain model by challenging terms, inventing edge cases, and capturing decisions in CONTEXT.md and ADRs.
actual_capabilities: Defines CONTEXT.md glossary format for documenting domain terminology
Defines ADR (Architecture Decision Record) template and numbering conventions
Guides when to offer an ADR based on reversibility, surprise, and trade-offs
Challenges ambiguous terms during conversation and proposes canonical alternatives
Cross-references glossary definitions against actual codebase behavior
limitations: Only produces markdown documentation files (CONTEXT.md, ADRs), no code generation
Requires human judgment to resolve terminology disputes
Does not enforce glossary consistency across the codebase automatically
Multi-context inference may require user clarification when boundaries are unclear

use_cases.title: Starting a new domain model
use_cases.description: Use when beginning a new project to establish the initial vocabulary and document key architectural choices before code is written.

use_cases.title: Resolving terminology conflicts
use_cases.description: Use during team discussions when different team members use different words for the same concept and clarity is needed.

use_cases.title: Documenting architectural decisions
use_cases.description: Use when making significant technology choices that future developers will need to understand, such as database selection or integration patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Begin domain modeling
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build the domain model for this project. Start by reading CONTEXT.md (if it exists) and then actively challenge any terminology I use as we discuss the design.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve terminology ambiguity
prompt_templates.prompt: I just used the term "account" in our discussion. Check CONTEXT.md and tell me if this conflicts with our defined vocabulary, then propose the precise term we should use.

prompt_templates.title: Stress-test domain boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: We just defined the relationship between Order and Invoice. Invent 3 edge-case scenarios that probe the boundary between these two concepts and force us to be precise.

prompt_templates.title: Record an architectural decision
prompt_templates.prompt: We just decided to use event sourcing for the write model. Check if this meets all three ADR criteria (hard to reverse, surprising without context, real trade-off). If yes, create the ADR using the format from ADR-FORMAT.md.

output_examples.input: User says: 'When a customer cancels their order, we need to refund the payment.'
output_examples.output: Response: "Your glossary defines 'cancellation' as ending an order before fulfillment, but refund is a billing concept. Are you cancelling the order and triggering a refund event in Billing, or is cancellation itself a billing operation? Let's pin down which context owns this."

output_examples.input: User decides to use PostgreSQL for the write model in an event-sourced system.
output_examples.output: Response: "This qualifies for an ADR — hard to reverse (database choice), surprising without context (why Postgres over an event store?), real trade-off (considered alternatives). Creating ADR-0003-postgres-for-write-model.md now."

best_practices: Create CONTEXT.md and ADRs lazily — only when you have something meaningful to record
Challenge every ambiguous term immediately rather than letting confusion accumulate
Keep CONTEXT.md definitions to one or two sentences focused on what the term IS
Only write ADRs for decisions that are hard to reverse, surprising without context, or involve real trade-offs
anti_patterns: Treating CONTEXT.md as a spec or scratch pad for implementation details
Writing ADRs for obvious decisions that have no real alternatives
Adding general programming concepts (timeouts, error types) to the glossary
Batching up terminology updates instead of capturing them inline as they are resolved

faq.question: What is the difference between CONTEXT.md and an ADR?
faq.answer: CONTEXT.md is a glossary of domain terms and their precise definitions. An ADR records a specific architectural decision, why it was made, and what alternatives were considered.

faq.question: When should I create an ADR?
faq.answer: Only when all three criteria are met: the decision is hard to reverse, it would surprise a future reader without context, and there were genuine alternatives with a real trade-off.

faq.question: Does this skill work with multi-context repos?
faq.answer: Yes. If a CONTEXT-MAP.md exists at the root, the skill reads it to find all contexts and their locations, then infers which context a discussion relates to.

faq.question: Can this skill generate code?
faq.answer: No. This skill produces only markdown documentation files (CONTEXT.md, ADRs). It does not generate or modify source code.

faq.question: How does the skill handle conflicting terminology?
faq.answer: When you use a term that conflicts with the existing glossary, the skill calls it out immediately and asks which definition is correct before proceeding.

faq.question: What file structure does this skill expect?
faq.answer: Single-context repos have CONTEXT.md at the root. Multi-context repos have a CONTEXT-MAP.md at the root pointing to context-specific files. ADRs live in docs/adr/ with sequential numbering.
